primary_dx: Hepatocellular Carcinoma, Trabecular/Nested Type, With Extensive Geographic Necrosis And Hemorrhage
differential_dx: Poorly Differentiated Intrahepatic Cholangiocarcinoma / Combined HCC-CCA

microscopic_findings.text: Viable tumor forms **thickened trabeculae** and **nests** with intervening **sinusoid-like spaces**.
Tumor cells are **polygonal** with **abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm**, round-oval nuclei, and conspicuous nucleoli.
There is extensive **geographic coagulative necrosis** with prominent **hemorrhage**, often directly abutting viable carcinoma.
Examined viable areas lack **gland formation**, **mucin**, and convincing **desmoplastic stroma**.

interpretation: The viable neoplasm shows **trabecular/nested hepatocellular architecture** with **polygonal eosinophilic cells** and sinusoid-like spaces, supporting **hepatocellular carcinoma**. The lesion is extensively replaced by **geographic necrosis and hemorrhage**, consistent with a largely necrotic tumor. The absence of **true gland formation**, mucin, or convincing desmoplastic stroma argues against poorly differentiated cholangiocarcinoma/combined HCC-CCA and makes metastatic hepatoid adenocarcinoma less favored on this material.

tiles.description:
The image shows a transition from a cellular area with a dense infiltrate to a large area of necrosis and hemorrhage. The cellular area contains cells with small, dark nuclei and scant cytoplasm, arranged in sheets and nests.

The most likely diagnosis is a poorly differentiated carcinoma, such as hepatocellular carcinoma or cholangiocarcinoma, given the cellular morphology and necrosis in the liver. Other possibilities include metastatic carcinoma or a high-grade sarcoma, which would require further evaluation with immunohistochemistry to distinguish.
tiles.rationale: Low-power survey upper-left periphery where thumbnail shows heterogeneous red/necrotic area.

tiles.description:
The image shows a low-power view of a liver lesion with a cellular proliferation of small cells with hyperchromatic nuclei and scant cytoplasm, arranged in sheets and nests with areas of necrosis and hemorrhage. The lesion appears to infiltrate the surrounding liver parenchyma.

The most likely diagnosis is a poorly differentiated hepatocellular carcinoma. The cellular morphology and infiltrative pattern are consistent with a malignant neoplasm of hepatocellular origin. The presence of necrosis and hemorrhage is also common in aggressive tumors.

Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can present with similar morphology and necrosis. Immunohistochemical stains for hepatocellular markers (e.g., HepPar1, Arginase-1) and markers for common metastatic sites (e.g., CK7, CK20, TTF-1) would be helpful to confirm the diagnosis and rule out metastasis.
tiles.rationale: global low-power scan for necrosis/hemorrhage vs viable liver and any nodular architecture

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of hepatocytes arranged in thickened cords and nests with intervening sinusoids. The cells have eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei, with areas of necrosis and hemorrhage.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which would typically show different cellular morphology and growth patterns, and benign hepatocellular lesions, which would lack the thickened cords and necrosis.
tiles.rationale: Low-power survey upper central area to assess viable rim vs diffuse necrosis.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular neoplasm with extensive areas of hemorrhage and necrosis. The tumor cells are arranged in sheets and nests with a prominent sinusoidal pattern.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can also present as a cellular neoplasm with necrosis, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation.
tiles.rationale: Left-sided paler region on thumbnail—possible necrosis/abscess wall

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular neoplasm with a prominent sinusoidal pattern and areas of necrosis. The cells are relatively uniform with round to oval nuclei and scant cytoplasm.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma, given the sinusoidal pattern and cellular morphology. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can also present with a sinusoidal pattern, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation.
tiles.rationale: Low-power survey upper-right periphery.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular neoplasm with a prominent sinusoidal pattern and areas of hemorrhage and necrosis. The cells are relatively uniform with round to oval nuclei and eosinophilic cytoplasm.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can also present with a sinusoidal pattern, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation.
tiles.rationale: right side parenchyma appears more uniform; look for subtle infiltrative vascular channels

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular neoplasm with a prominent trabecular pattern and areas of necrosis. The cells have eosinophilic cytoplasm and round to oval nuclei.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can also present with a trabecular pattern, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation.
tiles.rationale: Low-power survey lower-left including pale area suggestive of necrosis/abscess.

tiles.description:
The image shows a transition from a cellular area with a trabecular pattern to a less cellular, necrotic area with hemorrhage and inflammation. The cellular component consists of cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. The trabecular pattern and cellular morphology are characteristic of this tumor type. The necrotic and hemorrhagic areas are consistent with the gross description of a necrotic tumor mass.

Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can also present with a trabecular pattern, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation. Immunohistochemical stains for hepatocellular markers (e.g., HepPar1, Arginase-1) and biliary markers (e.g., CK7, CK19) would help differentiate these entities.
tiles.rationale: Upper-left area with apparent hemorrhage/congestion

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular neoplasm with a solid growth pattern and areas of necrosis. The cells have eosinophilic cytoplasm and round to oval nuclei, arranged in sheets and nests.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can have a similar appearance, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation.
tiles.rationale: near superior central pale linear cleft; check for infarct/tear/necrotic tract

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular neoplasm with a predominant trabecular pattern and areas of necrosis. The cells have round to oval nuclei and moderate eosinophilic cytoplasm.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can also present with a trabecular pattern, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation.
tiles.rationale: Central-lower region—look for cavitation, necrotic debris, inflammation

tiles.description:
The image shows a low-power view of a liver lesion with a thickened capsule and a cellular proliferation arranged in a trabecular pattern. The cells have eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei, with areas of hemorrhage and necrosis.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can also present as a liver mass with a trabecular pattern, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation.
tiles.rationale: Right upper region—compare for viable liver vs necrosis

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei, arranged in nests and cords with a prominent sinusoidal pattern. There is also a large area of amorphous eosinophilic material.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. The differential diagnosis includes metastatic carcinoma, which can also present with a sinusoidal pattern, but the polygonal cell morphology and eosinophilic cytoplasm are more characteristic of hepatocellular carcinoma.
tiles.rationale: hemorrhagic left-upper area; evaluate for blood lakes/peliosis-like spaces and vascular channel architecture

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei arranged in nests and cords, with areas of necrosis and hemorrhage.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma.

Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can have similar morphology, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation.
tiles.rationale: Viable-appearing cellular area adjacent to necrosis/hemorrhage—assess architecture (trabeculae vs glands) and stroma.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular neoplasm with a predominant trabecular pattern and areas of necrosis. The cells have eosinophilic cytoplasm and round to oval nuclei with visible nucleoli.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which would typically show different architectural patterns and cytologic features, and cholangiocarcinoma, which usually forms glandular structures.
tiles.rationale: Paler left-inferior area—evaluate for abscess cavity/necrotic debris/inflammation

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei, arranged in thickened cords and nests separated by sinusoidal spaces. There is also a prominent area of necrosis.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. The thickened cords and nests of polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei, along with the sinusoidal pattern, are characteristic features of hepatocellular carcinoma. The presence of necrosis is also common in malignant tumors.

Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can sometimes mimic hepatocellular carcinoma, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation and desmoplastic stroma, features not seen in this image.
tiles.rationale: Area described as thickened cords/nests with sinusoids—confirm HCC-like trabecular pattern and exclude glands.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in thickened trabeculae and nests separated by thin fibrovascular septa. The cells are relatively uniform with round to oval nuclei and moderate eosinophilic cytoplasm.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma, trabecular pattern. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can also form trabeculae, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular or ductal differentiation.
tiles.rationale: central area near cleft/tear; look for necrosis with viable rim and any anastomosing vascular channels

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords with intervening hemorrhage and necrosis. The cells have round to oval nuclei with prominent nucleoli and moderate eosinophilic cytoplasm.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. The differential diagnosis includes metastatic carcinoma, which can have similar morphology, but the clinical context of a liver mass and the cellular features are more consistent with a primary liver tumor.
tiles.rationale: Interface hemorrhage/necrosis—look for neutrophils, granulation tissue

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei, arranged in nests and cords with prominent sinusoidal spaces. Areas of hemorrhage and necrosis are also present.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which would typically show different cellular morphology and lack the characteristic sinusoidal pattern.
tiles.rationale: right-lower region with hemorrhage streaks; assess for infiltrative vascular tumor pattern

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords with intervening stroma. The cells have round to oval nuclei with finely granular chromatin and moderate eosinophilic cytoplasm. There is also evidence of necrosis and hemorrhage.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. The differential diagnosis includes metastatic carcinoma, which can have a similar nested pattern, but the cellular morphology and clinical context favor a primary liver tumor.
tiles.rationale: Upper-right viable tumor with sinusoidal pattern—look for endothelial wrapping, bile, pseudoacini.

tiles.description: The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords separated by thin fibrovascular septa. The cells have abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and round to oval nuclei with prominent nucleoli.
tiles.rationale: Lower-left tumor with trabecular pattern—check for inflammation/abscess features vs tumor.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei, arranged in thickened cords and nests separated by thin fibrovascular septa. Scattered clear spaces are also present.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma.

Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can have a similar trabecular pattern, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation.
tiles.rationale: Transition zone noted—characterize necrosis type and inflammation

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in thickened trabeculae and nests separated by thin fibrovascular septa. The cells have round to oval nuclei with finely granular chromatin and visible nucleoli, and abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. The thickened trabecular pattern and cellular morphology are characteristic of this tumor. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can also form nests and trabeculae, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation.
tiles.rationale: Lower-central trabecular tumor near hemorrhage—assess for macrotrabecular HCC vs cholangiocarcinoma.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei arranged in nests and cords, with prominent areas of hemorrhage and necrosis.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma.

Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can also present with a similar cellular morphology and necrosis, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation.
tiles.rationale: edge of hemorrhagic left-side zone; search for true vascular channels lined by atypical endothelium

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of large cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and prominent nucleoli, arranged in nests and cords, with extensive areas of necrosis and hemorrhage.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma.

Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can also present with large cells and necrosis, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation.
tiles.rationale: high power on suspicious hemorrhagic area for endothelial lining/hobnailing/atypia

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords with a prominent sinusoidal pattern. The cells have round to oval nuclei and abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm, with areas of necrosis and hemorrhage.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which would typically show different architectural patterns and cytologic features, and cholangiocarcinoma, which usually forms glandular structures.
tiles.rationale: Right upper cellular area—check for viable tumor vs inflammatory mass

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei, arranged in thickened cords and nests separated by sinusoidal spaces. The cells exhibit mild nuclear pleomorphism and prominent nucleoli.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma, given the hepatoid morphology and architectural pattern. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can mimic hepatocellular carcinoma, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation.
tiles.rationale: high power in central area to distinguish HCC-like trabeculae from vascular neoplasm; look for endothelial markers morphologically (intracytoplasmic lumina, RBC engulfment)

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords with a prominent sinusoidal pattern. The cells have round to oval nuclei with finely granular chromatin and moderate eosinophilic cytoplasm. There is a delicate fibrovascular stroma.

The morphologic features are consistent with a hepatocellular neoplasm. Given the clinical history of a liver mass and the histological appearance, hepatocellular carcinoma is the most likely diagnosis. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can sometimes mimic hepatocellular carcinoma, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation.
tiles.rationale: left-lower tumor/necrosis interface; evaluate for infarct vs abscess vs vascular neoplasm

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei, arranged in thickened trabeculae and nests separated by thin fibrovascular septa. The cells exhibit mild nuclear pleomorphism and prominent nucleoli.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma, trabecular pattern. The differential diagnosis includes metastatic carcinoma, which can also form trabeculae, but the polygonal cell morphology and eosinophilic cytoplasm are more typical of hepatocellular origin.
tiles.rationale: High power on representative viable trabecular tumor to assess cytology, bile, endothelial pattern.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei, arranged in thickened cords and nests separated by sinusoidal spaces. There is also evidence of necrosis.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. The differential diagnosis includes metastatic carcinoma, which can have a similar appearance but typically lacks the sinusoidal pattern and hepatocellular differentiation.
tiles.rationale: High power on tumor adjacent to necrosis to evaluate differentiation and exclude glands/mucin.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in thickened trabeculae and nests separated by thin fibrovascular septa. The cells have round to oval nuclei with finely granular chromatin and moderate eosinophilic cytoplasm.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. The thickened trabecular pattern and cellular morphology are characteristic of this tumor. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can also form nests and trabeculae, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular or ductal differentiation.
tiles.rationale: central trabecular/sinusoidal area; check for anastomosing channels, endothelial atypia, blood lakes

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in thickened trabeculae and nests separated by thin fibrovascular septa. The cells have round to oval nuclei with finely granular chromatin and moderate eosinophilic cytoplasm. Prominent sinusoidal spaces are present.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. The thickened trabecular pattern, cellular morphology, and prominent sinusoidal spaces are characteristic features of this tumor. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can sometimes mimic HCC, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation and desmoplastic stroma, features not seen here.
tiles.rationale: Follow linear pale/cleft-like area near midline—could represent necrotic tract/sinus

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of large cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and prominent nucleoli, arranged in nests and cords. There is also a large area of amorphous eosinophilic material.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. The large cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and prominent nucleoli, arranged in nests and cords, are characteristic features of hepatocellular carcinoma. The presence of necrosis is also common in this tumor type.

Differential diagnoses to consider include metastatic carcinoma, particularly from a primary site with eosinophilic cytoplasm such as renal cell carcinoma or adrenal cortical carcinoma. However, the arrangement in nests and cords and the cellular morphology are more consistent with hepatocellular carcinoma. Immunohistochemical stains for hepatocellular markers (e.g., HepPar1, Arginase-1) and markers for other potential primary sites would be helpful in confirming the diagnosis.
tiles.rationale: high power in right region to assess cytology; rule out epithelioid angiosarcoma features

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of polygonal cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei, arranged in nests and cords separated by thin fibrovascular septa. The cells exhibit mild nuclear pleomorphism and prominent nucleoli.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. The differential diagnosis includes metastatic carcinoma, which can have a similar appearance, but the polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and the arrangement in nests and cords are more characteristic of hepatocellular carcinoma.
tiles.rationale: High power on nested/cord pattern with fibrovascular septa—look for pseudoacini, bile, inflammation.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in thickened trabeculae and nests separated by thin fibrovascular septa. The cells have round to oval nuclei with finely granular chromatin and eosinophilic cytoplasm, with some areas showing clear cell change.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. The thickened trabecular pattern and cellular morphology are characteristic features. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can also form nests and trabeculae, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation.
tiles.rationale: Higher power of same tract—look for granulation tissue, neutrophils, organisms

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of large cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and prominent nucleoli, arranged in nests and cords with intervening stroma. There is also evidence of necrosis.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can have similar morphology, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation.
tiles.rationale: High power on upper-right tumor focus—exclude cholangiocarcinoma/sarcomatoid change.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords with intervening stroma. The cells have round to oval nuclei with finely granular chromatin and moderate eosinophilic cytoplasm. Areas of necrosis and hemorrhage are also present.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. The cellular morphology and architectural pattern are consistent with a primary liver malignancy. The presence of necrosis and hemorrhage is also common in hepatocellular carcinoma.

Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can also present with a nested or cord-like pattern. However, the cellular features and the clinical context of a liver mass favor hepatocellular carcinoma. Immunohistochemical stains for hepatocellular markers (e.g., HepPar1, Arginase-1) and markers for common metastatic sites (e.g., CK7, CK20, TTF-1) would be helpful in confirming the diagnosis.
tiles.rationale: right-mid region where parenchyma appears more preserved; look for infiltrative vascular tumor pattern or peliosis-like spaces

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of large cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and prominent nucleoli, arranged in thickened trabeculae and nests separated by thin fibrovascular septa. There is also evidence of necrosis.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. The differential diagnosis includes metastatic carcinoma, which can have similar morphology but typically lacks the trabecular pattern and sinusoidal spaces seen here.
tiles.rationale: Search at tumor-necrosis interface for neutrophilic microabscesses or bacteria

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords with moderate eosinophilic cytoplasm and pleomorphic nuclei. There is also a prominent area of necrosis and hemorrhage.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can have similar morphology, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation.
tiles.rationale: Left-lower hemorrhagic/necrotic focus—look for bacterial colonies, liquefaction

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei, arranged in thickened cords and nests separated by thin fibrovascular septa. The cells have a hepatoid appearance.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatoid carcinoma. This diagnosis is supported by the hepatoid morphology of the cells and their arrangement in cords and nests, which is characteristic of hepatocellular carcinoma. The clinical history of a liver mass and the presence of necrosis are also consistent with this diagnosis.

Differential diagnoses include hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) and metastatic carcinoma with hepatoid differentiation. HCC would typically show a more prominent sinusoidal pattern and may have bile production. Metastatic carcinoma with hepatoid differentiation would require clinical correlation and potentially immunohistochemical studies to confirm the primary site.
tiles.rationale: Previously requested lower-right area (not returned earlier) to check for alternative morphology; if viable then go 20x.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in thickened trabeculae and nests, with cells exhibiting eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei. Prominent sinusoidal spaces and areas of necrosis are also present.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which would typically show different cellular morphology and growth patterns, and cholangiocarcinoma, which usually forms glandular structures.
tiles.rationale: sample more central parenchyma away from hemorrhagic edges; look for interface with residual hepatic plates or vascular channel infiltration

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei, arranged in thickened cords and nests separated by thin fibrovascular septa. The cells exhibit mild nuclear pleomorphism and prominent nucleoli.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma, moderately differentiated. The thickened trabecular pattern and cellular morphology are characteristic of this tumor type. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can mimic HCC, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation.
tiles.rationale: central-lower area near hemorrhage streak; assess for infarct-like coagulative necrosis vs tumor necrosis, and any vasoformative pattern

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords with a prominent sinusoidal pattern. The cells have round to oval nuclei with finely granular chromatin and visible nucleoli, and abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm. There is also a large area of amorphous eosinophilic material.

The morphologic features are consistent with a hepatocellular neoplasm. The presence of a prominent sinusoidal pattern and cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm are characteristic of hepatocellular carcinoma. The large area of amorphous eosinophilic material likely represents necrosis, which is common in malignant tumors.

Differential diagnoses to consider include metastatic carcinoma, particularly from a primary site with eosinophilic cytoplasm such as renal cell carcinoma or adrenal cortical carcinoma. However, the prominent sinusoidal pattern and the clinical context of a liver mass favor a primary hepatocellular neoplasm. Immunohistochemical stains for hepatocellular markers (e.g., HepPar1, Arginase-1) and markers for potential metastatic sites would be helpful in confirming the diagnosis.
tiles.rationale: Central-lower area with hemorrhage—evaluate for inflammation/organisms

tiles.description:

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. The differential diagnosis includes metastatic carcinoma, which can mimic HCC, but the cellular morphology and arrangement are more typical of hepatocellular origin.
tiles.rationale: upper-right region not yet sampled densely; evaluate for peliosis-like spaces/anastomosing channels

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma with extensive necrosis.

Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma with necrosis or a large area of infarcted benign liver tissue.
tiles.rationale: Unexplored far upper-left edge—look for capsule, inflammation, abscess wall

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. The polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and the arrangement in nests and cords with sinusoidal spaces are characteristic features of hepatocellular differentiation. The presence of necrosis is also common in hepatocellular carcinoma.

Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can sometimes mimic hepatocellular carcinoma, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation. Immunohistochemical stains for hepatocellular markers (e.g., HepPar1, Arginase-1) and markers for other potential primary sites would be helpful in confirming the diagnosis.

Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can sometimes mimic hepatocellular carcinoma, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation and a different growth pattern. The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma.

Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can have a similar appearance, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation.
tiles.rationale: Unexplored top-central area—evaluate for surface necrosis, capsule, tract

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of polygonal cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei, arranged in thickened cords and nests separated by thin fibrovascular septa. The cells exhibit mild nuclear pleomorphism and prominent nucleoli.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. The differential diagnosis includes metastatic carcinoma, which can have a similar trabecular pattern, but the cellular morphology and eosinophilic cytoplasm are more typical of hepatocellular origin.
tiles.rationale: Top-central high power—search for acute inflammation/organisms

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords with intervening sinusoidal spaces. The cells have round to oval nuclei and moderate eosinophilic cytoplasm, with areas of hemorrhage and necrosis.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which would typically show different architectural patterns and cellular morphology, and cholangiocarcinoma, which usually forms glandular structures.
tiles.rationale: Bottom-central area—potential hemorrhage/necrosis; assess for abscess wall and inflammation

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei, arranged in nests and cords with intervening sinusoidal spaces. There is also an area of necrosis and hemorrhage.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. The differential diagnosis includes metastatic carcinoma, which can have a similar appearance, but the polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and sinusoidal pattern are more characteristic of hepatocellular carcinoma.
tiles.rationale: Bottom-left large largely unexplored area on map—look for necrotic debris/inflammation suggesting abscess vs tumor necrosis

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei, arranged in nests and cords. There is a prominent inflammatory infiltrate and areas of necrosis.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can have similar morphology, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation.
tiles.rationale: Same bottom-left area high power—search for neutrophils, bacterial colonies, granulation tissue

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords with a prominent sinusoidal pattern. The cells have round to oval nuclei with visible nucleoli and moderate eosinophilic cytoplasm. There is also a large area of necrosis.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. The differential diagnosis includes metastatic carcinoma, which can also present with a sinusoidal pattern, but the cellular morphology and clinical context favor a primary liver tumor.
tiles.rationale: Midline region near previously under-sampled—assess for portal tracts/bile duct injury or thrombosis

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation arranged in nests and cords with a prominent desmoplastic stroma. The cells have abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm, round to oval nuclei with prominent nucleoli, and exhibit moderate pleomorphism.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. The differential diagnosis includes metastatic carcinoma, which can also present with a desmoplastic stroma and cellular pleomorphism.
tiles.rationale: Higher power midline—evaluate inflammation type; look for organisms

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of cells with round to oval nuclei and eosinophilic cytoplasm arranged in nests and cords, with areas of necrosis and hemorrhage. The cells have a relatively uniform appearance.

The most likely diagnosis is a well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor (carcinoid tumor) of the liver.

Differential diagnoses include hepatocellular carcinoma, which typically shows more prominent nucleoli and a trabecular pattern, and metastatic carcinoma, which would depend on the primary site and may show different cytologic features.
tiles.rationale: Bottom-right quadrant—check for infarct-like coagulative necrosis or abscess wall

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei, arranged in nests and cords, with areas of necrosis and hemorrhage.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma.

Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can have similar morphology, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation.
tiles.rationale: Clarify unexpected neuroendocrine suggestion at bottom-right—confirm tumor type and check for inflammation/necrosis pattern

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei, arranged in nests and cords with intervening sinusoidal spaces. There is also a dense, eosinophilic, acellular layer at the periphery.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. The differential diagnosis includes metastatic carcinoma, which can have a similar appearance, but the presence of sinusoidal spaces and the clinical context of a liver mass favor a primary hepatic neoplasm.
tiles.rationale: High power bottom-right—look for neutrophils/bacteria, nuclear features

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei, arranged in sheets and nests. There is also a dense inflammatory infiltrate and areas of necrosis.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can have similar morphology, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation.
tiles.rationale: Area with reported prominent inflammation—characterize type (neutrophils vs lymphoplasmacytic) and search organisms

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of large cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and prominent nucleoli, arranged in thickened cords and nests. There is also significant necrosis and hemorrhage.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which can have similar morphology, and cholangiocarcinoma, which typically shows glandular differentiation.
tiles.rationale: Surface irregular/inflamed area—evaluate for abscess wall/sinus tract/granulation tissue

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular proliferation of polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei, arranged in thickened cords and nests separated by sinusoidal spaces. There is also evidence of necrosis.

The most likely diagnosis is hepatocellular carcinoma. Differential diagnoses include metastatic carcinoma, which would typically show different cellular morphology and growth patterns, and cholangiocarcinoma, which would have glandular differentiation.
tiles.rationale: High power of surface area—search for bacteria colonies or amoebae
